profile.name: Sarah Chen
conditions: Menorrhagia (since stopping birth control 6 months ago)
social_history: Occupation: Software engineer, sits at a desk all day
Tries to run but experiences cramps that make it difficult
patterns: Heavy menstrual bleeding associated with severe pelvic pain and lower back pain.
Ibuprofen provides temporary relief but wears off after a few hours.
Iron supplements can worsen nausea during menstruation.
Pain during sex is worsening and occurs in multiple instances.
Pain is often described as deep ache or sharp/stabbing.
Pelvic pain and back pain often occur in the days leading up to menstruation (premenstrual).
Pelvic pain often starts days before menstruation (premenstrual).
Pelvic pain severity increases during menstruation (e.g., 8/10 on day 2, 7/10 on day 3).
Pelvic pain severity increases during menstruation.
Periods have been getting worse and worse since stopping the pill.
Stopped birth control pill about six months ago due to mood side effects.
Symptoms like pelvic pain, back pain, bloating, and exhaustion tend to worsen around the time of menstruation (premenstrual).
Symptoms like pelvic pain, back pain, bloating, and exhaustion tend to worsen around the time of menstruation.
Symptoms like pelvic pain, back pain, bloating, fatigue, and heavy menstrual bleeding worsen cyclically around the time of menstruation.
Takes ibuprofen a lot for cramps.
profile.health_summary: Patient experiences cyclical worsening of pelvic pain, lower back pain, bloating, fatigue, and heavy menstrual bleeding in the days leading up to and during menstruation. Pain during sex is also a recurring issue that appears to be worsening.

intake_responses.agent_question: Hi! I'm your health tracking assistant. What's your name?
Nice to meet you! To help personalize your health tracking, how old are you and what's your biological sex?
To personalize tracking, do you have any chronic conditions?
intake_responses.patient_answer: Nothing diagnosed officially, but my periods have gotten really bad since I stopped birth control six months ago. And mild anemia.
parsed_items: Menorrhagia (since stopping birth control 6 months ago)

intake_responses.agent_question: Okay, thanks for sharing that. Now, shifting gears slightly, do you have any allergies to medications or foods?
intake_responses.patient_answer: No, none that I know of.

intake_responses.agent_question: Okay, great. Since you don't have any known allergies, let's move on to your regular medications or supplements. Are you currently taking anything like vitamins, over-the-counter drugs, or prescription medicines?
intake_responses.patient_answer: Just a daily multivitamin, iron supplement for the anemia — 65 milligrams — and ibuprofen when the cramps get bad.

intake_responses.agent_question: Okay, thank you. Now, shifting gears slightly, have you had any major surgeries or procedures in the past?
Okay, thank you. Now, let's talk about your family's health history. Are there any significant medical conditions that run in your family, like heart disease, diabetes, or certain types of cancer?
intake_responses.patient_answer: My mom had endometriosis actually, she had a hysterectomy at 40. I've been thinking about that.

intake_responses.agent_question: Thanks for sharing that. Now, shifting gears a bit, could you tell me about your lifestyle habits, such as smoking, alcohol use, or exercise?
intake_responses.patient_answer: No smoking or drinking really. I'm a software engineer, I sit at a desk all day. I try to run but lately the cramps make it hard.
parsed_items: Occupation: Software engineer, sits at a desk all day
Tries to run but experiences cramps that make it difficult

intake_responses.agent_question: You mentioned the cramps sometimes prevent you from running. Are there specific times of the month or certain activities that seem to trigger or worsen your menorrhagia and cramps?
intake_responses.patient_answer: I stopped the pill about six months ago because of mood side effects. Since then the periods have been getting worse and worse. I take ibuprofen a lot for it.
parsed_items: Periods have been getting worse and worse since stopping the pill.
Takes ibuprofen a lot for cramps.
Stopped birth control pill about six months ago due to mood side effects.

intake_responses.agent_question: Besides the menorrhagia, anemia, and iron supplementation you mentioned, are there any other ongoing health conditions or concerns I should be aware of?
intake_responses.patient_answer: I'm 29, generally healthy except for these period problems. The anemia is from the heavy bleeding. I've been to two different doctors and they just say go back on the pill.
parsed_items: Generally healthy except for period problems. Anemia is from heavy bleeding.

log_responses.transcript: Period started this morning. Really heavy flow and the cramps are brutal — like a 7 out of 10. Took ibuprofen 400mg. My periods have been so much worse since I went off birth control six months ago.

extracted.transcript: Period started this morning. Really heavy flow and the cramps are brutal — like a 7 out of 10. Took ibuprofen 400mg. My periods have been so much worse since I went off birth control six months ago.

agent_response.acknowledgment: Starting your period with heavy flow and strong cramps again sounds really tough, especially since they've been worse lately after stopping birth control.
agent_response.immediate_question: You mentioned heavy flow — you're on Day 1 of your cycle (menstrual phase). Have you noticed this symptom at a similar point in your cycle before?

log_responses.followup_answer: Honestly yes — the heavy flow and cramps are always worst on days 1 and 2. It's been like this every month since I went off birth control. The ibuprofen 400mg helps a little but not enough.
log_responses.followup_response: Thanks for clarifying that the heavy flow and cramps are typical for days 1 and 2 of your cycle, even though they've been worse since stopping birth control. It's understandable that this pattern is frustrating, especially when the ibuprofen isn't fully effective.

log_responses.transcript: Day 2 of my period. Still super heavy — went through a pad in two hours this morning. My lower back hurts so much I can barely sit at my desk. Pain is like an 8. The ibuprofen wears off after three hours.

agent_response.acknowledgment: That must be really rough, Sarah. Heavy bleeding and severe back pain on day 2 are definitely impacting your ability to work.

log_responses.followup_answer: It's deep, not muscular — radiates from my pelvis. It's so bad I can barely focus at work.
log_responses.followup_response: Thanks for sharing that, Sarah. It sounds incredibly difficult to manage pain radiating from your pelvis while trying to focus on work, especially when it's deep and not muscular. I understand how frustrating that must be.

log_responses.transcript: Period is finally winding down, just spotting now. Cramps dropped to maybe a 2. Feeling more human. Took my multivitamin and iron with dinner.

log_responses.followup_answer: Yeah, much better today! First day I haven't needed ibuprofen. I'm just tired from the whole week.

log_responses.transcript: Feeling great today! No pain, no bloating, tons of energy. I actually went for a 5K run this morning — oh wait, did I take my iron? Yeah I took my iron and my multivitamin with breakfast. But yeah, why can't every day be like this?

extracted.transcript: Feeling great today! No pain, no bloating, tons of energy. I actually went for a 5K run this morning — oh wait, did I take my iron? Yeah I took my iron and my multivitamin with breakfast. But yeah, why can't every day be like this?

agent_response.acknowledgment: It's great to hear you're feeling so well today! It sounds like you're really enjoying the increased energy. While I can't say for sure why your periods are sometimes difficult and others not, it's common for menstrual symptoms to fluctuate, especially after stopping hormonal birth control. The heavy bleeding leading to anemia is a known factor that can impact your overall energy levels when it occurs.

log_responses.followup_answer: Honestly the contrast is wild. Five days ago I was curled up with a heating pad and today I ran 5K. It's like I'm two different people depending on where I am in my cycle.
log_responses.followup_response: Thanks for sharing that, Sarah. It sounds incredibly frustrating to experience such a stark difference in how you feel depending on where you are in your cycle. I understand how disheartening it must be to go from feeling great and active to being sidelined by pain.

log_responses.transcript: Sharp, stabbing pain in my lower right side that came on suddenly. Kind of scared me — it was like a 5 for about an hour and then faded. My husband asked if I should go to urgent care but it went away.

agent_response.immediate_question: Since this is a new type of pain for you and occurred mid-cycle (Day 14), do you think it might be related to ovulation or something else?
agent_response.question_rationale: The patient describes a sudden onset, sharp, stabbing pain that resolved. This is different from her usual menorrhagia/cramps. It's important to explore the nature and potential cause of this new symptom, especially given its timing in the cycle.

log_responses.followup_answer: I don't think so? This is the first time I've had pain like that mid-cycle. It was really sudden — lower right side, sharp. Gone after maybe an hour. Should I be worried? I'm around day 14 I think.
log_responses.followup_response: Thanks for clarifying that this pain is new and different from your usual cycle, especially since it was sudden and sharp. Given its location and intensity, even though it resolved, it's understandable to be concerned, particularly with a family history of endometriosis. It might be worth discussing this episode with your doctor to rule out any underlying issues.

log_responses.transcript: Starting to feel bloated and just... heavy. My whole lower belly feels swollen. Also exhausted — couldn't finish my evening run, had to walk the last mile. Is this PMS already? It's only day 22 of my cycle.

extracted.transcript: Starting to feel bloated and just... heavy. My whole lower belly feels swollen. Also exhausted — couldn't finish my evening run, had to walk the last mile. Is this PMS already? It's only day 22 of my cycle.

agent_response.acknowledgment: It's understandable to wonder if these symptoms are related to PMS, especially given that you're on day 22 of your cycle. Bloating, feeling heavy/swollen, and exhaustion are common premenstrual symptoms. Since stopping birth control six months ago, you've experienced menorrhagia (heavy periods) and mild anemia, which can sometimes be associated with hormonal fluctuations around the cycle.
agent_response.immediate_question: How would you rate your exhaustion on a scale of 1 to 10, where 1 is barely noticeable and 10 is completely debilitating?
agent_response.question_rationale: Severity assessment helps gauge the impact of symptoms and track changes over time.

log_responses.followup_answer: Maybe a 6? I couldn't even finish my run. And the bloating just kind of crept up today — my jeans were tight and I hadn't eaten anything unusual. It's so frustrating.

log_responses.transcript: I feel awful. Pelvic pain is an 8, exhausted, nauseous. Had to cancel dinner with friends — second time this month. Took ibuprofen 600mg. I actually went to my GP last week about the pain and she said 'cramps are normal, take ibuprofen.' That's literally all I've been doing and it's not working.

agent_response.immediate_question: Taking 600mg ibuprofen with iron while feeling nauseous can increase GI bleeding risk — have you noticed any dark stools or stomach pain? Worth discussing this combination with your doctor.

log_responses.followup_answer: No dark stools, no. My stomach hurts but I think that's just the cramps and nausea, not bleeding. I actually saw my GP last week — she said everything felt normal, suggested the pill or naproxen. When I pushed back she said some women just have bad periods. I got a gyno referral but it's six weeks out. Six weeks of this.
log_responses.followup_response: Thanks for clarifying about the stomach pain and lack of dark stools. It's completely understandable to feel frustrated when your GP dismissed your concerns, especially since this is impacting your life significantly and you have a long wait for the gynecology referral.

log_responses.followup_answer: Yes, every single month. The pelvic pain ramps up the last few days before my period, like clockwork. My GP suggested going back on the pill or trying naproxen instead of ibuprofen. But I can't go back on the pill — the mood side effects were ruining my life. And switching painkillers doesn't explain why this is getting worse every month since I stopped birth control. Nobody seems to think that's worth investigating.
log_responses.followup_response: Thanks for sharing that, Sarah. It really sounds frustrating when your pain is predictable but worsening, and it feels like your concerns about the change since stopping birth control aren't being fully addressed. I understand why you're looking for answers beyond just managing symptoms with medication.

log_responses.transcript: Period day 3. Still heavy bleeding. Back pain is awful, maybe a 7. I'm going through ibuprofen like candy — took 600mg this morning. Starting to think something is actually wrong with me. This isn't normal period stuff, right?

extracted.transcript: Period day 3. Still heavy bleeding. Back pain is awful, maybe a 7. I'm going through ibuprofen like candy — took 600mg this morning. Starting to think something is actually wrong with me. This isn't normal period stuff, right?

agent_response.acknowledgment: You're really concerned about the intensity of your period symptoms. Given that you stopped birth control 6 months ago, it's understandable to wonder if this is a change from what was normal for you before. Heavy bleeding and severe pain can sometimes be related to changes after stopping hormonal contraception, but it's also important to consider other potential causes, especially since you mentioned feeling like something might be wrong. It's always worth discussing significant changes with your doctor. Over the past month, you've logged heavy bleeding and pain (severity 6-8) on days 1-4 of your cycle, consistently taking ibuprofen. This pattern suggests persistent discomfort during menstruation.

log_responses.transcript: Just got back from the gynecologist. She took a full history, did a pelvic exam — said she could feel some tenderness and nodularity that concerned her. Then I showed her the app data and she spent a while going through the symptom patterns across my two different cycle lengths. She said it's very consistent with endometriosis. She's ordering a transvaginal ultrasound and wants me back in two weeks to review. She also brought up trying continuous hormonal therapy for symptom relief while we work this up — I told her about the mood issues with the pill and she said there are other options we can discuss after imaging. I cried in the parking lot. Not sad crying. Just... someone finally took me seriously.

She's ordering a transvaginal ultrasound and wants me back in two weeks to review. She also brought up trying continuous hormonal therapy for symptom relief while we work this up — I told her about the mood issues with the pill and she said there are other options we can discuss after imaging. I cried in the parking lot. Not sad crying. Just... someone finally took me seriously.

log_responses.followup_answer: She actually examined me properly — first doctor to do a real pelvic exam for this. Said she could feel some things that concerned her. Then she looked at the app data and said the cyclical pattern across different cycle lengths was characteristic. She's doing the ultrasound, and depending on what that shows, we'll talk about next steps — could be hormonal management, could be laparoscopy, depends. She said without the tracking data she probably would have started with 'let's monitor this for a few months,' which I've already done. So the data meant we didn't have to start from zero. That's what got me. I've been fighting for months to be heard and it took one doctor who actually looked.

doctor_packet.hpi: A 29-year-old female with a history of menorrhagia presents with cyclical lower abdominal and pelvic pain that has been worsening over the past several months. The pain typically begins approximately one week before her expected menses and escalates over several days, peaking around the time of menstruation. Onset was gradual but has become more severe recently. The location is primarily in the pelvis and lower back. Duration varies but lasts for several days during her period. Characterized as sharp, stabbing (right side) or deep, aching pain. Aggravating factors include movement and intercourse (deep dyspareunia). Relieving factors are minimal; ibuprofen provides little relief at current severity levels. Timing is cyclical, related to her menstrual cycle. Severity has increased significantly: initially around 5/10 on 2026-02-02, worsening to 7/10 by 2026-02-04, and reaching 8/10 by 2026-02-06. Associated symptoms include heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia), nausea, bloating, exhaustion, and lower back pain. The pain is so severe that it interferes with her daily activities, including work and sleep. She reports taking ibuprofen 400mg at the onset of her period, which helps a little initially but becomes ineffective as the pain worsens. She also took naproxen 1000mg on 2026-02-05 with inadequate relief.
pertinent_positives: Severity increasing from 5/10 to 8/10 over several days around menses
Associated symptoms: menorrhagia, nausea, bloating, exhaustion, lower back pain (VAS 6/10)
Deep dyspareunia (severity 6/10 reported on 2026-01-24, worsening trend implied)
Pain onset ~1 week prior to menses
Poor response to ibuprofen 400mg and naproxen 1000mg
Functional impact: interferes with daily activities, work, sleep

questions_for_clinician: Why are my periods getting so much worse? I feel like it's out of control.
Is this pain normal? It feels different and way more intense than before.
What if this keeps getting worse? Will I ever be able to have a normal life or relationship?
I took naproxen 1000mg and it barely touched the pain. What else can I do when nothing works?
Could this be something serious like endometriosis, even though I don't know much about it? Is that why my periods are so bad and painful?
Why does sex hurt so badly now? It wasn't always like this.
